sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 3, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 2 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I understand what happened:
- Node 32 (gray container) had its boundary change - it now has a notch/cutout on the right side
- Node 34 (light green pixel) moved from [[40,10],[41,11]] to [[40,12],[41,13]] - it moved 2 columns to the right
- Node 32 lost node 34 as a child (34 is no longer fully enclosed)
- The gray container's boundary now shows a dent on the right side where the green pixel moved out
So RIGHT moves the selected/active object to the right. The light green pixel inside the gray container moved right by 2 pixels. The container's shape changed to accommodate this.
But wait - which object is "selected"? It seems like the light green pixel (node 34) was the active one. Or maybe the gray container was selected and the green pixel inside it moved.
Let me think about this differently. The gray container (node 32) had a child (node 34, light green). After RIGHT, the green pixel moved right by 2 columns and is no longer a child of the gray container. The gray container's boundary changed to show a notch.
This suggests that arrow keys move objects. The question is: which object is being moved? It seems like the light green pixel was the "active" one. But how was it selected?
Maybe the last MOUSE click selected the yellow pixel, but the arrow keys moved a different object? Or maybe the selection moved from yellow to green somehow?
Actually, wait - let me reconsider. The first action was MOUSE on yellow pixel (row 20, col 24). Then RIGHT was pressed. The green pixel moved. Maybe the selection isn't on the clicked object but on some default object, or maybe the selection cycles.
Let me try pressing RIGHT again to see if the same object moves further, or try other directions.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# RIGHT moved the light green pixel (node 34) 2 cols to the right, out of its gray container
# Let me try RIGHT again to see if it keeps moving, and try other directions

# First let's check current state of node 34
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = {n['id']: n for n in seg['nodes']}

print(f"Node 34 (green) current: boundary={nodes[34]['boundary']}, px={nodes[34]['pixels']}")
print(f"Node 32 (gray container) current: boundary={nodes[32]['boundary']}, children={nodes[32].get('children')}")

# Try RIGHT again to see if green pixel moves further
action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"\nAfter 2nd RIGHT: {last_action_result}")

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
nodes2 = {n['id']: n for n in seg2['nodes']}
print(f"Node 34 after: boundary={nodes2[34]['boundary']}")

# Now try LEFT to see if it reverses
action(['LEFT'])
print(f"\nAfter LEFT: {last_action_result}")

seg3 = current_frame.segmentation
nodes3 = {n['id']: n for n in seg3['nodes']}
print(f"Node 34 after LEFT: boundary={nodes3[34]['boundary']}")

# Try UP
action(['UP'])
print(f"\nAfter UP: {last_action_result}")

seg4 = current_frame.segmentation
nodes4 = {n['id']: n for n in seg4['nodes']}
print(f"Node 34 after UP: boundary={nodes4[34]['boundary']}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "Moves the active object right by 2 pixels. The light green pixel (node 34) was the active object.", "MOUSE": "Clicking selects objects, advances timer"}, "object_taxonomy": {"light_green": "Small 2x2 movable object", "gray_container": "4x8 container that holds objects"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "White horizontal bar at row 63, shrinks over time"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Arrow keys move an active object. Light green pixel moved right when RIGHT pressed. Object moves 2 pixels per keypress.", "goal_model": "Likely move colored objects to match targets on the right side", "recent_findings": ["RIGHT moved light green pixel 2 cols right, out of its container", "Container boundary changed to show notch where pixel exited"], "open_questions": ["Which object is currently active?", "How does selection work?", "What is the goal configuration?"], "current_plan": "Test arrow key behavior systematically", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["MOUSE on yellow pixel - only advanced timer"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
